hero.role: Assistant Professor · Intelligent systems applied to health and active ageing
hero.bio: Assistant Professor (Profesor Ayudante Doctor) and researcher at the University of Jaén, with ANECA accreditation as Lecturer (Profesor Permanente Laboral). My work sits at the intersection of IoT, fuzzy logic and deep learning, with a focus on connected health, chronic-patient monitoring and active ageing.

biography.title: Biography
biography.p1: José Luis López Ruiz (Linares, Jaén, 1993) holds a B.Sc. in Computer Engineering (2018), an M.Sc. in Computer Engineering with the Best Master's Thesis Award (2020) and a Ph.D. in Information and Communication Technologies (2023), all from the University of Jaén. He currently serves as Assistant Professor (Profesor Ayudante Doctor) in the Department of Computer Science and is a member of the ASIA (TIC-257) research group at the same university. He has held the ANECA accreditation as Lecturer (Profesor Permanente Laboral) since December 2023. His research lines combine Internet of Things, fuzzy logic and deep learning in connected-health and active-ageing systems.
biography.p2: My academic career has developed entirely at the University of Jaén, where I started in 2019 as research staff under the Youth Guarantee programme. Between 2020 and 2022 I held a position as R&D&I Support and Management Technician, and in February 2022 I joined university teaching as Interim Substitute Lecturer. The defence of my doctoral thesis in May 2023, followed by the ANECA accreditation in December of the same year, led to my contract as Substitute Lecturer. Since December 2024 I hold an Assistant Professor (Profesor Ayudante Doctor) position in the Computer Architecture and Technology area. In 2025 I was awarded a José Castillejo grant from the Spanish Ministry of Science, Innovation and Universities for a three-month research stay (May–July 2026) at Ulster University (Belfast, United Kingdom).
biography.p3: My research is currently organised in four complementary lines, all focused on intelligent systems applied to health and active ageing. The first one, started during my doctoral thesis, tackles human activity recognition using IoT sensors deployed in domestic and care environments, aimed at monitoring older adults and chronic patients. A second line, derived from the first, addresses glucose monitoring and management of type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus, combining treatment-adherence platforms with the generation of linguistic summaries from sensor data through fuzzy logic and large language models. In parallel I work on indoor positioning with BLE and UWB technologies, applied mainly to assisted-living environments. More recently, and under my own leadership, I have opened a fourth line on stress detection from biometric data in low-cost IoMT devices, whose first results appear in Computer Methods and Programs in Biomedicine (Q1, IF 4.8). Across these four lines, I maintain continuous work on efficient IoT/IoMT architectures and the assessment of their alignment with the Sustainable Development Goals.
